macro_segments.title: The Status of the Kohen as Owner of the Bechor: Foundational Principles

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Who is Considered the Owner (Ba'al HaKorban) for Performing Temurah
The Mishnah's Position on Bechor: Kohen Cannot Perform Temurah Despite Eating the Meat
The Intrinsic Sanctity of Bechor and Its Implications for Ownership
Establishing the Principle: The Owner, Not the One Who Instills Kedushah, Performs Temurah

macro_segments.title: The Sale of Bechor: Distinguishing Nowadays from the Time of the Beit HaMikdash

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah from Ma'aser Sheni: When Bechor Can Be Sold (Alive, Blemished, or Dead)
Why Bechor Cannot Be Redeemed But Can Be Sold: The Nature of Intrinsic Sanctity
Demonstrating Ownership Through Marriage: Can a Kohen Use Bechor as Kesef Kiddushin?
Rav Nachman's Distinction: Nowadays vs. When the Beit HaMikdash Stands
The Gemara's Challenge: If Nowadays Kohen is Owner, How Does the Mishnah Apply?
Resolution: The Mishnah Discusses Nowadays, Not Implying Anything About Beit HaMikdash Times

macro_segments.title: Proving the Kohen's Non-Ownership in Beit HaMikdash Times: The Pasuk and Bayit Aleinu

micro_segments.title: The Bayit Aleinu Case: Selling Unblemished Bechor Must Occur in Beit HaMikdash Times
Resolving Ambiguity: The Pasuk Discusses Blemished Animals, Not Unblemished

macro_segments.title: The Taruva Case: When Ownership Status Prevents the Beit HaMikdash from Seizing the Animal

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Two Men Mixed Up at Birth—One Kohen, One Slave—With an Unblemished Bechor
The Halacha: They Wait for a Blemish Because Either Might Be the Kohen Owner
The Proof: If Kohen Were Not the Owner, the Beit HaMikdash Would Seize the Animal
The Gemara's Response: Nowadays Everyone Waits for a Blemish; the Chiddush is Monetary Law

macro_segments.title: The Ir HaNidachat Case: Whether Unblemished Bechor is Destroyed with Idolatrous City's Property

micro_segments.title: Context: Property of an Idolatrous City Must Be Destroyed Unless It Belongs to Heaven
The Question: Is Unblemished Bechor (Owned by Kohen) Part of the City's Property to Destroy?
The Resolution: Even Blemished Bechor Is Excluded Because Its Kedushah Prevents Normal Trade

macro_segments.title: The Ashám Case: Trespass Against God When Denying a Friend's Sanctified Property

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose HaGlili's Position: Kodashim Kalim Are the Property of Owners (Mamon Ba'alim)
Ben Azzai's Limitation: Only Bechor (Not All Kodashim Kalim) Is Fully Owned
Why Bechor Alone? It Lacks the Extra Trappings and Restrictions of Shelamim and Ma'aser
The Gemara's Analysis: We Are Discussing Beit HaMikdash Times and an Unblemished Bechor
Abaye's Answer: This Discussion Applies to Chutz La'aretz, Where Bechor Is Never Offered

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri vs. Rabbi Akiva: Can the Kohen Perform Temurah on His Own Bechor?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri's Challenge: Bechor Is Different—Kohen Has Rights While Animal Lives
Abaye's Resolution: We Are Discussing Chutz La'aretz, Where Kohen Is the Owner
The Gemara's Reframing: This Debate Parallels a Tannaitic Dispute About Temurah by Kohen or Owner

macro_segments.title: Practical Restrictions: To Whom May a Kohen Sell His Bechor?

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Position: A Kohen May Sell Bechor Only to Another Kohen, Not to a Yisrael
The Concern: A Yisrael Might Artificially Create a Blemish and Falsely Blame the Kohen
The Safeguard: Sages Will Not Rule on a Yisrael's Bechor Without the Kohen Present
Story: Mar Zutra Challenges Rav Ashi's Household on Eating Meat from a Sold Bechor
The Servants' Defense: We Paid for It, So It's Not an Inappropriate Exchange
Rav Huna's Concern Revisited: The Real Worry Is That a Discounted Sale Encourages Future Dealings
Final Resolution: A Kohen Won't Give Discounts on Hope of Future Bechor; Immediate Profit Rules

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri
hamotzi mechavero alav haraya (burden of proof)
